class_schedule.topic: Global Environmental Governance: The Institutional Landscape
learning_objectives: Map the architecture of international environmental governance
Analyze the tragedy of the commons as the central challenge
Evaluate the effectiveness of multilateral environmental agreements
readings: Chasek, Downie & Brown, Global Environmental Politics Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Environmental problems are the quintessential collective action problems in international relations. No state can solve them alone, yet cooperation is structurally difficult.
key_concepts: Tragedy of the commons: individual rationality producing collective irrationality
Common pool resources vs. public goods
MEAs: from Stockholm (1972) to Rio (1992) to Paris (2015)
UNEP, CBD, UNFCCC: the institutional architecture
Compliance and enforcement in environmental regimes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Commons simulation: a resource extraction game demonstrating overexploitation dynamics

review.value: Hardin's concept that shared resources are depleted when individuals act in self-interest, even though cooperation would benefit all -- central to understanding environmental governance
A treaty among three or more states addressing environmental issues, with over 500 MEAs currently in force covering climate, biodiversity, chemicals, desertification, and more

class_schedule.topic: Climate Change: Science, Politics, and the Paris Agreement
learning_objectives: Summarize the scientific basis for climate change
Analyze the political dynamics of UNFCCC negotiations
Evaluate the Paris Agreement's architecture and effectiveness
readings: Falkner, 'The Paris Agreement and the New Logic of International Climate Politics'

lecture_notes.introduction: Climate change is the defining environmental challenge of our era. The Paris Agreement represents a new approach but faces enormous implementation challenges.
key_concepts: Climate science basics: greenhouse effect, feedback loops, tipping points
UNFCCC regime evolution: Kyoto Protocol to Paris Agreement
Paris Agreement architecture: NDCs, global stocktake, ratchet mechanism
Common but differentiated responsibilities
Loss and damage: the most contentious issue
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing and comparing Nationally Determined Contributions from five countries

review.value: Voluntary climate pledges submitted by each country under the Paris Agreement, representing a bottom-up approach replacing Kyoto's top-down targets
The UNFCCC principle recognizing that all states share responsibility for the environment but developed countries bear greater responsibility due to historical emissions

learning_objectives: Analyze the causes and consequences of global biodiversity loss
Evaluate the Convention on Biological Diversity and Kunming-Montreal framework
Assess conservation strategies: protected areas, payments for ecosystem services
readings: IPBES Global Assessment Report (Summary for Policymakers excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Biodiversity loss is the other great environmental crisis, intertwined with climate change but receiving far less political attention. The consequences for human well-being are profound.
key_concepts: Sixth mass extinction: rates and drivers
Ecosystem services: provisioning, regulating, cultural, supporting
CBD and the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework
Protected areas: 30x30 target
Indigenous and local community conservation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Ecosystem services valuation exercise: calculating the economic value of a wetland

review.value: The benefits humans derive from ecosystems: food and water (provisioning), climate regulation and flood protection (regulating), recreation and spiritual value (cultural), nutrient cycling (supporting)
The Kunming-Montreal GBF goal to protect 30% of land and ocean areas by 2030, a landmark commitment requiring massive expansion of protected areas

class_schedule.topic: Water Scarcity, Oceans, and Transboundary Environmental Issues
learning_objectives: Evaluate the law of the sea and marine environmental protection
Assess the politics of shared resources: rivers, aquifers, oceans
readings: UNCLOS Part XII: Protection of the Marine Environment (selected articles)

lecture_notes.introduction: Water and oceans are shared resources that cross borders. Managing them cooperatively is one of the most practical and urgent challenges in environmental governance.
key_concepts: Transboundary water governance: rivers, lakes, aquifers
Water wars debate: scarcity as driver of conflict or cooperation
UNCLOS: maritime zones, EEZs, deep seabed mining
Ocean governance: fisheries, pollution, marine protected areas
The high seas treaty: BBNJ Agreement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Negotiation simulation: upstream and downstream states negotiating a river-sharing agreement

review.value: The management of rivers, lakes, and aquifers shared by multiple states, requiring cooperation through treaties, joint commissions, and benefit-sharing arrangements
The 2023 High Seas Treaty creating a legal framework for conserving biodiversity in areas beyond national jurisdiction, including marine protected areas and environmental impact assessments

learning_objectives: Analyze the political economy of energy transition
Evaluate climate justice frameworks: equity, vulnerability, historical responsibility
Assess the role of non-state actors in environmental governance
readings: Newell & Mulvaney, 'The Political Economy of the Just Transition'
Mary Robinson Foundation, Principles of Climate Justice

lecture_notes.introduction: The transition from fossil fuels to clean energy is the most significant economic transformation in human history. How it is managed raises fundamental questions of justice.
key_concepts: Energy transition politics: incumbents, innovators, and losers
Climate justice: procedural, distributive, recognition
Loss and damage: compensation for climate impacts
Corporate climate action: greenwashing vs. genuine transformation
Cities, subnational actors, and non-state climate governance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Stakeholder analysis: mapping winners and losers of coal phase-out in a developing country

review.value: The framework recognizing that climate change disproportionately harms those who contributed least to it, demanding equitable distribution of both climate burdens and transition benefits
The principle that the shift to a green economy must protect workers and communities dependent on fossil fuels through retraining, social protection, and economic diversification

class_schedule.topic: The Future of Global Environmental Governance
learning_objectives: Assess the adequacy of current environmental governance for future challenges
Analyze the role of technology and innovation in environmental solutions
readings: Gates, How to Avoid a Climate Disaster Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Current environmental governance was designed for a world of sovereign states. Planetary boundaries require governance innovations that transcend traditional approaches.
key_concepts: Planetary boundaries framework: nine boundaries, safe operating space
Earth system governance: polycentric, networked, adaptive
Geoengineering: governance of last-resort technologies
Green technology transfer and intellectual property
Youth climate activism and intergenerational equity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Institutional design exercise: creating a governance framework for solar geoengineering

review.value: Rockstrom et al.'s framework identifying nine Earth system processes with boundaries that, if crossed, risk irreversible environmental change; several boundaries already exceeded
Ostrom's concept of governance through multiple overlapping centers of authority rather than a single global institution, applied to climate and environmental governance

questions.question: Explain the 'tragedy of the commons' and how it applies to climate change. What features of the Paris Agreement attempt to overcome this collective action problem?
questions.answer: The tragedy of the commons occurs when individuals acting in self-interest deplete a shared resource, even though cooperation would benefit all. Climate change is a global commons problem because the atmosphere is shared: each country benefits from emitting greenhouse gases (economic growth) while the costs (warming) are shared by all. The Paris Agreement addresses this through: (1) universal participation -- all countries submit NDCs, creating peer pressure; (2) the ratchet mechanism requiring progressively ambitious pledges; (3) transparency framework for monitoring and reporting; (4) global stocktake to assess collective progress. However, NDCs remain voluntary and current pledges are insufficient, showing the tragedy persists.

rubric.key: Explains tragedy of the commons clearly
Applies it to climate change specifically
Identifies Paris Agreement mechanisms to address it

questions.question: What does 'common but differentiated responsibilities' mean in climate governance? Why is this principle both essential and contentious?
questions.answer: CBDR recognizes that all states share responsibility for protecting the global environment but developed countries bear greater responsibility because they historically produced most greenhouse gas emissions and have greater financial and technological capacity to act. It is essential because without it, developing countries would refuse to participate -- asking poor countries to bear equal costs for a problem created by rich countries would be unjust and politically impossible. It is contentious because: emerging economies like China and India are now major emitters but claim developing country status; developed countries argue that future emissions matter more than historical ones; and the binary developed/developing distinction poorly captures the diversity of national circumstances.

questions.question: What are ecosystem services and why is their economic valuation important for environmental policy? Identify one risk of relying too heavily on economic valuation.
questions.answer: Ecosystem services are the benefits humans derive from natural ecosystems, categorized as provisioning (food, water), regulating (climate, flood control), cultural (recreation, spiritual), and supporting (nutrient cycling, soil formation). Economic valuation is important because it makes the costs of environmental destruction visible to policymakers and markets -- when ecosystem services are treated as free, they are overexploited. Putting a price on them can justify conservation investments (e.g., the cost of preserving a wetland is less than the cost of building a flood control system). Risk: reducing nature's value to monetary terms may undermine intrinsic value arguments, exclude non-economic values (spiritual, cultural), and allow wealthy actors to 'pay to pollute' rather than change behavior.

questions.question: Explain climate justice and its three dimensions (distributive, procedural, recognition). Why do developing countries demand a 'loss and damage' mechanism?
questions.answer: Climate justice is the framework addressing the ethical dimensions of climate change. Distributive justice concerns the fair allocation of climate burdens and benefits (who pays, who is protected). Procedural justice demands that those most affected have meaningful voice in decision-making. Recognition justice requires acknowledging the disproportionate impacts on marginalized communities and the historical responsibility of industrialized nations. Developing countries demand loss and damage because they are already suffering irreversible climate impacts (sea level rise, extreme weather) that adaptation cannot prevent, caused primarily by historical emissions from developed countries. Without a loss and damage mechanism, the poorest countries bear the costs of a crisis they did not create, violating all three dimensions of climate justice.

rubric.key: Defines climate justice with three dimensions

major_assignments.title: Environmental Policy Case Study and Recommendations
major_assignments.description: Select a specific global environmental issue and analyze the governance framework addressing it. Evaluate effectiveness and produce policy recommendations for strengthening the regime.
objectives: Analyze the governance framework for a specific environmental issue
Evaluate regime effectiveness using criteria from the literature
Communicate environmental policy analysis to a professional audience

criteria: Thorough understanding of the environmental problem
Evidence-based assessment of compliance and outcomes
Honest assessment of strengths and weaknesses
Comprehensive analysis of environmental governance regime

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on environmental science, governance, and policy

participation.description: Engagement in simulations, negotiations, and data exercises

major_assignments.description: Environmental policy case study and recommendations

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering climate, biodiversity, and governance frameworks

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam integrating all environmental topics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. More than 3 unexcused absences reduce participation grade by half.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 5% per day. Policy briefs cannot be submitted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Group simulation work is collaborative. All written analysis individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work original with proper citation. Misrepresenting environmental data results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to IPCC reports, UNEP databases, and environmental policy journals through university library.
